(N.S.\.), 665, reversed, ApreaL from a decision of the Supreme Court of New § Wales, refusing to grant a rule nisi for a new trial (1), The respondent brought an action against the appellants fy breach of a contract, by which the appellants agreed to sell to plaintiff, for twelve months from 2nd September, 1903, the whole of the appellants' output of spelter dross at £11 per ton, to delivered by the appellants from time to time as the dross produced at the works of the appellants. The appellants were manufacturers of galvanized iron and wire-netting, and the spelter dross was a valuable product over after the process of passing the iron or wire through galvanizing tanks. The breach of contract alleged in the declaration was a refus to deliver the dross as agreed. 'The appellants pleaded in the first instance (1) non assump (2) denial of respondent's readiness and willingness, and (3 denial of the breaches. : appellants, in the course of an audit of their books, made cet discoveries as to the circumstances surrounding the m of the contract, which pointed to the conclusion that the con in had been procured by the respondent acting in fraudulent collusion with the appellants' general manager, one Wilkinson. text: Rule 64 of the Rules of the Supreme Court 'spmetically a transcript of the English rule 6 of 'Trinity 'Term 1853. It provides: " In all actions on simple contraet, except as inafter excepted, the plea of non asswmpsit, or a plea traversing the contract or agreement. alleged in the declaration, shall uperate only as a denial in fact of the express contract, promise, agreement alleged, or of the matters of fact from which the t, promise, or agreement alleged may be implied by law." That plea put the 'spondent upon the proof of all that was Messsary to establish the fact of the contract between himself nd the appellants. 'The contract in question was entered into, 'tot by the appellants themselves, under their seal, but by a person to be their agent. Now, when an action is brought by a phintiff aguinst a defendant on a contract, he must prove that j_ uutmet was made, and, if the contract was made by an agent, Hemust prove the authori 'y of the agent to make it.

text: Having held out the age his agent to make contracts of that kind, he cannot set up, ag a person dealing innocently with the agent on the faith of holding out, that the agent has in fact gone beyond the limits d 'is authorit What a plaintiff undertakes to prove in a case ort is set out very clearly in a considered judgment of th Court of Exchequer in the case of Reynell v. Lewis (1) In delivering judgment, Pollock O.B. said (2 "The question, in all cases in which the plaintiff seeks to fi the defendant with liability upon a contract express or impli is whether such contract was made by the defendant, by hi or his agent, with the plaintiff or his agent, and this is a ques of fact for the decision of the jury upon the evidenee before "The plaintiff, on whom the burden of proof lies in all cases, must, in order to recover against the defendant, show that he (the defendant) contracted expressly or impliedly; expres by making a contract with the plaintiff, impliedly, by giving a order to him under such circumstances as shew that it was m be gratuitously executed: and if the contract was not made ty the defendant personally, it must be proved that it was made an agent of the defendant properly authorized, and that it ms made as his contract." The learned Chief Baron then, pointing out how the ageney may be constituted, continued: " all these cases, if the agent, in making the contract, acts 0118 authority, the principal is bound by the contract, and the age contract is his contract, but not otherwise. This agency nay : (1) 16M. & W., 517. (2) 15 M. & W., 517, apo R] OF AUSTRALIA. ited by the immediate act of the party, that is, by really the authority to the agent, or representing to him that he Jo lave it, or by constituting that relation to which the law tigches agency; or it may be created by the representation of defendant to the plaintiff, that the party making the contract isthe agent of the defendant, or that such relation exists as to stitute him such ; and if the plaintiff really makes the contract nthe faith of the defendant's representation, the defendant is found; he is estopped from disputing the truth of it with respeet that contract; and the representation of an authority is, quoad we, precisely the same as areal authority given by the defendant the supposed agent." A little further on he says: " Upon none ofthese propositions is there, we apprehend, the slightest doubt." Clearly, therefore, by the denial of the contract all these matters re put im issue, I will again summarise what the plaintiff must we, He must prove that the person with whom he dealt was the agent of the defendant, and that, in making the contract, the agent was acting as the agent of the defendant.

text: Let me give an illustration of the difference. It is not enough that a man should le the agent of another to enable him to make any contract on his behalf; he must make it as his agent. For example: suppose the ease of a person authorized to sign a promissory note, per proc, and he makes a promissory note in payment of his own private debt and gives it to his ownereditor. Clearly that is not within his authority. 'The promissory note is not the note of his Principal, because the person taking it knows that the agent is tot acting for his principal, but for himself.
citation: Lysaght Bros and Co Ltd v Falk [1905] HCA 7

text: Again, the master ofa ship is agent for the shipowner, and may sign a bill of lading on behalf of the owner for goods, but if the master were to sign Fill of lading for goods not put on board the ship, he would tot be acting for the owner ; and although he had authority to bills of lading, a contract made by him in such a way is the contract of his principal. That was decided in the case of Grant v. Norway (1).
citation: Lysaght Bros and Co Ltd v Falk [1905] HCA 7

Braithwaite (1), decided by Eldon LC, i question was as to the authority of the agent by whom theq tract had been made, The Lord Chancellor said (2): "Whe aman is a general, or a special, agent, and, admitting the di ence of the principle governing the question, how much further one can bind the principal than the other can, it is impossi supposing a special agent can bind beyond his authority, to em tend, that if he made at the time a declaration that he had authority, the principal can be bound.

text: So in the ease of a genenl agent as an auctioneer, he may at the auction state, what limit tions are imposed on his general power of agency." That is an instance of a case where an agent tells the other party that heis acting beyond the scope of his authority. But the principle is the same. It is manifestly unimportant whether the agent tell the person with whom he is dealing that he has no authority to make the contract, or whether the circumstances under whieh he makes it are, to the knowledge of the other party, such as show that he had no such authority. In either case the agent: acting beyond the scope of his authority, and the other person knows it.

text: The rule was put very plainly by Lord Ksher MR, in The British Mutual Banking Co. v. Charnwood Forest Rail way Co. (3). That was an action against a company to recov damages for fraudulent misrepresentations, alleged to have been made by an agent of the company when acting within the seopeat his authority.

text: Lord Hsker said (4): "'The rule has often beet expressed in the terms, that to bind the prineipal the agent must acting 'for the benefit' of the principal. This, in my opinion, 3) 18 Q.B.D., TH. ry 18 Q.B.D., 714, at p77 OF AUSTRALIA. rent to saying that he must be acting 'for' the principal, if there is authority to do the act it does not matter if the inipal is benefited by it. text: If the agent has acted in his awn interest, he does not bind his employer. But there is an exception to this rule in the case of a person dealing bond fide with the agent without knowledge of the limitation of his suthority. 'That is based upon the principle of estoppel; but there ean be no estoppel if the person dealing with the agent knows the actual facts, and knows that the agent is acting in his own interests and not in the interests of his employer. That is 'nanifest from the case of Hambro v.

text: Burnand (1). The headnote of that case is: " Where an agent, in contracting on behalf of his principal, has acted within the terms of a written authority ven to him by the principal, but the existence of which was tut known to the other party to the contract, the principal can'ut, if the other party has acted bond. fide, repudiate liability on the contract on the ground that the agent, in making it, acted in his own interests, and not in those of his principal." 'The point ofthat is that the other party was acting bond fide. The prosition assumes that the agent, when acting in his own interests, as acting outside his authority, but, the other person not knowing that, the principle to which I have referred applies, and the principal is estopped from denying the authority of the agent.

If the person with wh the agent is dealing has a knowledge of circumstances which that the agent is acting in his own interest, and not in the interest of his principal, he cannot allege that there was authority, It is conceded that, if the facts alleged in this case, and of which the appellants sought to give evidence, were established, they would have shown that the alleged agent had no authority in fact, because he was acting for himself and the respondent, and not for the appellants, in making the contract. text: I now come to what I described as the point of form. Itis this. text: But oneat the facts which the respondent, as plaintiff, has undertaken to establish is the authority of the agent. If he fails to establish that, and if upon the evidence it appears that the agent had no authority, and that the respondent knew it, what differenee canit make whether, in addition to proving that the agent had no authority, the appellants proved that he was engaged in a fraudulent enterprise ? For his contention in that regard the respondent relies on r, 67, which is as follows :—" In every species of action on contract, all matters in confession and avoidance, including not only those by way of discharge, but those which show the trans action to be either void or voidable in point of law, on the ground of fraud or otherwise, shall be specially pleaded," and examples are given.

text: Now, what is the governing id of the latter rule? The rule is: [His Honor then read the and continued]. 'The governing idea is that there must be special plea wherever the contract alleged is admitted to haye been made in fact, but there is some reason based eitheron lay oupon the principles of equity, why the defendant should be bound by it. There is no mystery about the words fession and avoidance." text: It therefore seems to me that the first element whith must be present in any pleading in order to bring it under 67 is an admission of the statements of fact made in the pattiela pleading to which it is ananswer.
